From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1989 film by Uli Edel

***Last Exit to Brooklyn*** is a 1989 [drama film](/wiki/Drama_film "Drama film") directed by [Uli Edel](/wiki/Uli_Edel "Uli Edel") and adapted by [Desmond Nakano](/wiki/Desmond_Nakano "Desmond Nakano") from [Hubert Selby Jr.](/wiki/Hubert_Selby_Jr. "Hubert Selby Jr.")'s 1964 [novel of the same title](/wiki/Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn "Last Exit to Brooklyn").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The film is an [international co-production](/wiki/Co-production_(media) "Co-production (media)") between [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The story is set in 1950s Brooklyn and takes place against the backdrop of a [labor strike](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action"). It follows interlocking storylines among the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") underbelly of the [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn") neighborhood, including [unionized](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") workers, [sex workers](/wiki/Sex_worker "Sex worker"), and [drag queens](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1952 Brooklyn, workers have entered the sixth month of a [strike](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") against the local factory. [Shop steward](/wiki/Union_representative "Union representative") Harry Black relishes his new role as the strike secretary as it is more lucrative than his job and allows him to get out of the house, away from his wife who does not realize that Harry is [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay"). Boyce, the union leader, tries to negotiate between the factory and union representatives.

Meanwhile, Tralala is a prostitute who lures unsuspecting sailors out to a vacant lot to be robbed by Vinnie, an ex-convict and Tralala's [pimp](/wiki/Procuring_(prostitution) "Procuring (prostitution)"). Georgette is a young [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") woman who harbors a crush on Vinnie. Big Joe, one of the striking workers, learns that his daughter is pregnant out of wedlock by Tommy, one of the factory workers known for riding a motorcycle and confronts him at the strike office, leading to a fight. Joe's son admires Tommy and is saving for a motorcycle to impress Tralala. Tommy agrees to marry Joe's daughter, but the baby is born before the wedding. At the wedding party, Joe again starts a fight with Tommy, but they make peace when they realize their fighting is threatening the baby.

Harry meets Regina, another transgender woman, and falls in love with her. Sleeping at Regina's one morning, he misses the arrival of trucks at the factory, which force their way in. The strikers try to prevent the trucks from leaving, which escalates into a riot. The police disperse the riot with water cannons and tear gas and Harry is injured in a fight with one of the strikebreaking drivers, making him a hero to the strikers. Harry introduces Vinnie to Boyce, who takes up an offer from Vinnie to destroy the trucks of the contractor responsible, but he then fires Harry after discovering his abuse of expenses and his absence the morning the trucks arrived. Harry returns to Regina, who throws him out. Heartbroken, Harry is badly beaten by Vinnie and his associates, who encourage Georgette to join in.

Tralala also meets a kindly sailor in Manhattan who appears to truly love her and lets her move in with him. When he is eventually sent away to war, he leaves her a note expressing his hope that he will return from the war to rekindle their relationship. Depressed, she goes to one of her old haunts and drunkenly exposes her breasts to a mixed group of locals and sailors before encouraging the men to have sex with her. This escalates into a gang rape. Tralala is left catatonic on a mattress, where Joe's son finds her.

At Tommy's wedding, Boyce announces that management has caved to their demands and the factory will reopen on Monday, prompting celebration. On Monday, as they return to work, Joe eventually welcomes Tommy to the family and offers him marital advice if he ever needs it.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Peter Dobson](/wiki/Peter_Dobson "Peter Dobson") as Vinnie
* [Jerry Orbach](/wiki/Jerry_Orbach "Jerry Orbach") as Boyce

* [Frank Military](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Military&action=edit&redlink=1 "Frank Military (page does not exist)") as Steve
* Jason Andrews as Tony
* James Lorinz as Freddy
* [Sam Rockwell](/wiki/Sam_Rockwell "Sam Rockwell") as Al
* Maia Danziger as Mary Black
* [Camille Saviola](/wiki/Camille_Saviola "Camille Saviola") as Ella
* [Ricki Lake](/wiki/Ricki_Lake "Ricki Lake") as Donna
* Cameron Johann as Spook
* [John Costelloe](/wiki/John_Costelloe_(actor) "John Costelloe (actor)") as Tommy
* [Christopher Murney](/wiki/Christopher_Murney "Christopher Murney") as Paulie
* [Alexis Arquette](/wiki/Alexis_Arquette "Alexis Arquette") as Georgette
* Zette as Regina
* [Mark Boone Junior](/wiki/Mark_Boone_Junior "Mark Boone Junior") as Willie

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

There had been several attempts to adapt *Last Exit to Brooklyn* into a film since the controversial book's 1964 publication.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) One of the earliest attempts was made by producer [Steve Krantz](/wiki/Steve_Krantz "Steve Krantz") and animator [Ralph Bakshi](/wiki/Ralph_Bakshi "Ralph Bakshi"), who wanted to direct a live-action film based on the novel. Bakshi had sought out the rights to the novel after completing *[Heavy Traffic](/wiki/Heavy_Traffic "Heavy Traffic")*, a film which shared many themes with Selby's novel. Selby agreed to the adaptation, and actor [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") accepted the role of Harry in *Strike*. According to Bakshi, "the whole thing fell apart when Krantz and I had a falling out over past business. It was a disappointment to me and Selby. Selby and I tried a few other screenplays after that on other subjects, but I could not shake *Last Exit* from my mind."[[8]](#cite_note-8) An adaptation was also considered by [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") and [Brian de Palma](/wiki/Brian_de_Palma "Brian de Palma") at one point.[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5)

German producer [Bernd Eichinger](/wiki/Bernd_Eichinger "Bernd Eichinger") and director [Uli Edel](/wiki/Uli_Edel "Uli Edel") had been wanting to adapt the novel for 20 years, with the latter having first discovered the novel as a university student.[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9) The two filmmakers obtained the rights to Selby's novel in the mid-1980s after Edel's success with the film *[Christiane F](/wiki/Christiane_F._(film) "Christiane F. (film)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9)

One of the challenges of adapting Selby's book was combining its different stories and characters into one film.[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5)[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9) Screenwriter [Desmond Nakano](/wiki/Desmond_Nakano "Desmond Nakano") was brought on to pen the screenplay.[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filming")]

Filming took place over 14 weeks in the summer of 1988[[10]](#cite_note-SteveW-10) on location in the [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn") section of Brooklyn, "only blocks from the housing projects where Mr. Selby lived while writing *Last Exit.*"[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9)

Some scenes for the film were shot at Montero's Bar and Grill, which was owned by [Pilar Montero](/wiki/Pilar_Montero "Pilar Montero") and her husband.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

As the gang-rape scene was about to be shot, Jennifer Jason Leigh stood up before the mob of actors and said, "Don't forget, it's only acting." There was an awed silence, then the actors broke into applause. [[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Release")]

The film was first released in Europe in 1989, where it was a critical and a commercial success.[[9]](#cite_note-Sharkey-9) The film had a [limited distribution](/wiki/Limited_release "Limited release") in the United States in May 1990 after delays to its release date.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Some theaters, such as the [Edwards Theater](/wiki/Edwards_Theaters "Edwards Theaters") in [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California"), declined to show the film because of its dark and graphic subject matter.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Critical response")]

The film has a 67% rating on review aggregator website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") based on 21 critics' reviews.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigned the film a score of 62 out of 100, based on 21 critics, indicating "generally favorable" reviews.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Though critics noted the film's unrelenting bleakness and how it is not an easy watch, *Last Exit to Brooklyn* was also praised for Uli Edel's direction and the performances of its actors.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) [Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote the film "is both grim and eloquent. The strike scenes are some of the roughest ever seen in a fiction film."[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Canby added the film "has a European sensibility that works to the advantage of its American subject matter...[and] sees everything at the distance of a sober-minded alien observer. One result is that '*Last Exit to Brooklyn*' never appears to exploit its sensational subject matter."[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) He concluded the film "suggests that physical, social, psychological and political degradation can only be understood when seen in something like their true dimensions. Without hope."[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) In a review that awarded 3 and ½ stars out of four, [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") wrote the characters "are limited in their freedom to imagine greater happiness for themselves, and yet in their very misery they embody human striving. There is more of humanity in a prostitute trying to truly love, if only for a moment, than in all of the slow-motion romantic fantasies in the world."[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

[Sheila Benson](/wiki/Sheila_Benson "Sheila Benson") of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* wrote, "The strike-breaking attempt at the factory is *Last Exit*’s vast visual set-piece, a long and magnificently staged mass of moving bodies and machinery that shows Edel’s accomplished and painterly eye and the remarkable camera work of Stefan Czapsky."[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5) Benson singled out Jennifer Jason Leigh as the "defiantly tragic Tralala, [Ohrbach’s] *[sic]* implacable union leader, Stephen Lang’s self-hating Harry Black and Alexis Arquette’s dry wit-over-desperation as Georgette" as the film's standout performances.[[5]](#cite_note-Benson-5)

## Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Accolades")]

For her role as Tralala in this film and her role in *[Miami Blues](/wiki/Miami_Blues "Miami Blues")*, Jennifer Jason Leigh won the award for Best Supporting Actress from both the [New York Film Critics Circle](/wiki/1990_New_York_Film_Critics_Circle_Awards "1990 New York Film Critics Circle Awards")[[17]](#cite_note-NYFilm-17) and the [Boston Society of Film Critics](/wiki/Boston_Society_of_Film_Critics_Awards_1990 "Boston Society of Film Critics Awards 1990").[[18]](#cite_note-Kentucky-18)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Home media")]

*Last Exit to Brooklyn* was released on Blu-ray by [Summit Entertainment](/wiki/Summit_Entertainment "Summit Entertainment") in October 2011.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Last_Exit_to_Brooklyn_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: See also")]
